key_insight: A 2-D phase-field model shows that strong anisotropy in the TiO2 surface reaction rate is the main driver of the observed [001] nanowire morphology during H2-assisted nanocarving.
engineering_relevance: The results provide a practical orientation-control map for tuning TiO2 microstructures and guiding fabrication of aligned nanowire arrays.
method: Phase-field model of TiO2 nanocarving in H2-bearing gas
Includes TiO2 reduction, Ti3+ diffusion, and anisotropic surface energy, diffusivity, and reaction rate
Simulated single-crystal and polycrystal cases across grain misorientation angles
evidence_signal: Systematic simulations across carving stages and grain orientations were used to isolate the dominant anisotropy factor and generate a nanocarving morphology map.
watch_item: The study is 2-D and model-based, so 3-D effects, parameter calibration, and real gas/defect complexities may limit direct transfer to experiments.
paper_title: Phase-field modeling of TiO2 nanocarving via reaction with hydrogen-bearing gas
